blocks.body: Omitting Relative Pronouns shows you when who / which / that can disappear from a relative clause and when that would break the structure.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In some object relative clauses, the relative pronoun can be omitted. This makes the sentence shorter, but the rule does not work everywhere.","rules":[{"label":"Possible in object clauses","color":"emerald","text":"If the pronoun is the object, you can often leave it out.","example":"The book (that) I bought is expensive."},{"label":"Not possible in subject clauses","color":"blue","text":"If the pronoun is the subject, omission is usually impossible.","example":"The boy who sits there is my cousin."},{"label":"Meaning stays the same","color":"amber","text":"When omission is allowed, the meaning does not change - only the form becomes shorter.","example":"The song which I love = The song I love."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. When omission is possible
blocks.body:
You can omit the relative pronoun when there is already a subject after it and the relative pronoun is not the subject.

  • The film (that) we watched last night was excellent.
  • The person (who) I called didn't answer.
  • The bag (which) she bought is on the chair.

In every example, the subject inside the clause is we, I, or she.

blocks.heading: 2. When omission is not possible
blocks.body:
• Subject clause: The man who works here is friendly. - you cannot say The man works here is friendly.
  • After some clear preposition patterns in formal English, the relative pronoun usually stays: The colleague to whom I wrote...
  • For clarity, it is sometimes better to keep the pronoun even when omission is possible.

blocks.heading: 3. Compare the examples
blocks.body:
Full formOmitted formPossible?
The woman who I met yesterdayThe woman I met yesterdayyes
The car that he wants to buyThe car he wants to buyyes
The teacher who explains everything clearlyThe teacher explains everything clearlyno

Ask yourself: is there a separate subject after the pronoun? If yes, omission is often possible.

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: omitting the pronoun in a subject clause. This creates a broken sentence.
  • Mistake: thinking omission is obligatory. It is a style choice, not a rule you must use every time.
  • Mistake: losing the boundary between the main clause and the relative clause. If the verb comes straight after the noun without a new subject, the pronoun is probably needed.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Omission is usually possible in object relative clauses.
  • In subject relative clauses, we usually keep the pronoun.
  • Before omitting it, check whether the clause has its own subject after the pronoun.
  • When in doubt, keep the pronoun - it is safer and clearer.
